# Inference Rule

**A logical statement that derives a new fact from a set of existing premises.**

An inference rule specifies conditions under which a conclusion can be drawn.
In formal logic, rules take the form: given premises P1, P2, …, Pn, conclude C.

Inference rules are the mechanism by which a reasoning engine extends a
knowledge base without requiring explicit enumeration of every derivable fact.
They are fundamental to automated reasoning systems, expert systems, and
description logic reasoners such as those used in OWL ontologies.

Unlike a raw assertion, an inference rule encodes generalisable knowledge
rather than a specific fact about a specific individual.

Rules can be expressed in many formalisms including Horn clauses, SWRL rules,
SPARQL CONSTRUCT queries, and production rules in expert system shells.
